it: Vettori e matrici a dimensione infinita
en: Vectors and matrices of infinite dimension

en: Other images in this chapter

it: Schema di principio dell'esperimento.
en: Schematic of the experiment.

it: Struttura cristallina: reticolo tridimensionale di nuclei.
en: Crystal structure: three-dimensional lattice of nuclei.

it: Previsione classica: deflessione dell'elettrone nel cristallo.
en: Classical prediction: deflection of the electron in the crystal.

en: Photograph of the evacuated glass bulb.

en: Wiring diagram of the system.

en: The whole system powered up.

en: Image obtained on the screen: central spot and two circles.

it: Struttura cristallina piana della grafite.
en: Planar crystal structure of graphite.

it: Scomposizione del reticolo in sottoreticoli a linee parallele.
en: Decomposition of the lattice into parallel-line sublattices.

it: Formazione dei cerchi: diffrazione dal reticolo.
en: Formation of the circles: diffraction by the lattice.

it: Immagine prodotta da un singolo cristallo.
en: Image produced by a single crystal.

it: Sovrapposizione di cristalli orientati a caso: si formano i cerchi.
en: Superposition of randomly oriented crystals: the circles form.

it: Descrizione dell'esperimento e previsioni classiche.
en: Description of the experiment and classical predictions.

it: La figura 1 mostra uno schema di principio
en: Figure {0} shows a schematic of the experiment. We have a beam of electrons striking a thin foil of crystalline material; the electrons that cross the foil are detected on a phosphor screen.

it: La struttura di un cristallo verrà studiata
en: The structure of a crystal will be studied later, in the card on Rutherford's experiment and in the card on X-rays. We will see that a crystal consists of an aggregate of atomic nuclei arranged in a three-dimensional lattice (Fig. {0}), with a more or less complicated geometry. We will also see that the distance between two adjacent nuclei is much larger than the diameter of the nucleus.

it: Da un punto di vista classico ci aspettiamo che un elettrone
en: From a classical point of view we expect that an electron, when it crosses a crystal, is deflected by a certain angle and in a certain direction that depend on the initial motion of the electron and on the orientation of the crystal (Fig. {0}). A beam is made of many electrons, each with dynamical conditions slightly different from every other. So, beyond the crystal, we will have a spreading of the beam because not all the electrons are deflected by the same angle.

it: L'angolo di deflessione δ è una variabile aleatoria
en: The deflection angle δ is a random variable, that is, a quantity that can take different values, each with a certain probability. The probability distribution for the variable δ can be calculated on the basis of the corpuscular model of the electron, and on this model we expect a bell-shaped curve like the one shown in figure 4. But, as we will see, the distribution of electrons actually detected on the screen does not correspond to this classical prediction.

it: Descrizione dell'apparato sperimentale.
en: Description of the experimental apparatus.

it: Il cannone elettronico, la lamina di materiale cristallino
en: The electron gun, the crystalline-material foil and the phosphor screen are mounted inside an evacuated glass bulb.

it: La figura 5 mostra una foto di quest'ampolla
en: Figure {0} shows a photograph of this bulb, while figures {1} and {2} show the electron gun in detail.

it: Uno schema è rappresentato in figura 8.
en: A diagram is shown in figure {0}.

it: Come si vede dalla foto di figura 6
en: As can be seen from the photograph in figure {0} and the diagram in figure {1}, the crystal foil is placed directly on the final stage of the electron gun.

it: Il sistema si alimenta come mostrato in figura 9.
en: The system is powered as shown in figure {0}.

it: Gli alimentatori utilizzati sono due
en: Two power supplies are used: one for the high voltage of 0÷5000 V and one for the low voltages of 6 V and 0÷50 V. The photograph in figure {0} shows the whole system powered up. On the left is the high-voltage supply, and on the right the low-voltage supply. The bulb is mounted on a universal support.

it: Risultati sperimentali.
en: Experimental results.

it: La foto di figura 11 mostra in dettagli
en: The photograph in figure {0} shows in detail the image obtained on the screen.

it: Si osservano un punto centrale e due cerchi
en: One observes a central spot and two concentric circles. The probability distribution for the variable δ deduced from this image is the one shown in figure 12.

it: Se variamo la tensione di accelerazione si osserva
en: If we vary the accelerating voltage, the radii of the two circles are seen to change, that is, the deflection angles δ₁ and δ₂ change.

it: Sono state effettuate le seguenti misure:
en: The following measurements were taken:

it: La figura 13 riporta due grafici
en: Figure 13 shows two plots of the measurements taken: one with the voltage V on the ordinate, the other with the inverse square root of the voltage {0}. From the second plot one can observe that the angles δ₁ and δ₂ are directly proportional to the inverse square root of the voltage: {1} and {2}.

it: Interpretazione dei risultati.
en: Interpretation of the results.

it: La figura ottenuta sullo schermo non può essere spiegata
en: The image obtained on the screen cannot be explained if one thinks the electron gun fires a beam of classical material particles. It can be explained, instead, if one thinks the gun generates a wave similar to that produced by a LASER, but not electromagnetic. Indeed, according to this idea, the image formed on the screen can be interpreted as a diffraction pattern produced by the crystal lattice of the graphite contained in the foil placed in front of the beam.

it: La grafite ha una struttura cristallina piana
en: Graphite has a planar crystal structure shown in figure {0}. Within this lattice one can identify several sublattices of equally spaced parallel lines (Fig. {1}). The diffraction due to the original lattice is approximately equal to the superposition of the diffractions produced by the individual sublattices of equally spaced parallel lines.

it: Nel cristallo si individuano due tipi di reticoli
en: In the crystal one identifies two types of parallel-line lattices, one with spacing {0}, and the other with spacing {1}. So, when the beam crosses the lattice, we have two first-order diffraction angles: {2} corresponding to {3} and {4} corresponding to {5}.

it: I cerchi della figura 11 si formano
en: The circles in figure {0} form because the foil is made of many randomly oriented crystals:

it: Ogni singolo cristallo forma un'immagine
en: Each single crystal forms an image made of a few spots:

it: La sovrapposizione di tante immagini ruotate
en: The superposition of many images rotated by a random angle forms the circles:

it: Il fatto che gli angoli di diffrazione del primo ordine
en: The fact that the first-order diffraction angles depend on the accelerating voltage applied means that the wavelength of the beam depends on the voltage.

it: Dualismo onda particella e relazione di De Broglie.
en: Wave–particle duality and the De Broglie relation.

it: Il cannone elettronico spara una raffica di particelle
en: Does the electron gun fire a burst of particles, or does it generate a beam of waves? The experiment described in this card leads us to think it is a beam of waves, but other experiments lead us to the opposite answer. The result is that neither hypothesis is really correct: the electron is neither a wave nor a particle.

it: L'elettrone ha un comportamento corpuscolare
en: The electron has a corpuscular behaviour, in the sense that when it interacts with other systems it produces discrete effects, in packets. For example, in Millikan's experiment one observed the effects of a single electron or of a few electrons at a time; indeed one observed a discrete charge, in packets. There are also other experiments that show the corpuscular nature of the electron, but we will describe them later.

it: L'elettrone ha anche un comportamento ondulatorio
en: The electron also has a wave behaviour, because it can produce diffraction. It is also possible to show the interference of electrons, similar to that produced by light with two slits, but the experimental apparatus needed to observe this phenomenon includes an electron microscope, and it is rather difficult to find a teaching laboratory equipped with such a system.

it: Si conclude che quando pensiamo ad un elettrone
en: We conclude that when we think of an electron we should imagine a material particle accompanied by a wave. The wavelength is given by a formula found by De Broglie: {0}, where p is the momentum of the particle, λ is the wavelength and h is the Planck constant. This formula can be confirmed with our experiment and has a very general validity, in the sense that it holds for all types of particle — electrons, atomic nuclei, particles of light, and so on. The following table reports the experimental measurements we took; also reported are the wavelength {1}, calculated from the diffraction formula {2}, and the momentum {3}, calculated from the energy equation {4}.

it: Interpretazione probabilistica.
en: Probabilistic interpretation.

it: In questo paragrafo riprenderemo i concetti
en: In this section we will take up again the concepts introduced in the second card, to apply them to the electron.

it: Una particella materiale, dal punto di vista della Meccanica Quantistica
en: A material particle, from the point of view of Quantum Mechanics, is regarded as a physical system on which position measurements can be made. When we perform such a measurement, the result is in general not certain, but random. So we have a probability distribution {0} for the variable x. As we said in card two, the probabilities can be obtained as the squared moduli of certain complex numbers called probability amplitudes. So we have a certain complex function {1} and the probability is given by {2}.

it: La funzione ψ(x) è una distribuzione di ampiezze
en: The function {0} is a distribution of probability amplitudes and characterises the state of the particle; this function can be seen as a vector of infinitely many complex numbers, and we will represent it with the ket-vector symbol {1}.

it: Quando lo stato della particella evolve la funzione ψ cambia
en: When the state of the particle evolves, the function ψ changes with time, so we obtain a function that depends on space and time {0}, which we can represent with a time-dependent ket {1}.

it: Come abbiamo visto nella scheda due, l'evoluzione temporale
en: As we saw in card two, the time evolution is described by a linear law {0}. Here {1} is the vector associated with the function {2} representing the initial state; {3} is the vector associated with the function {4}, and {5} is the time-evolution matrix.

it: A questo punto si pone il problema di determinare la matrice
en: At this point the problem arises of determining the time-evolution matrix {0}. We will solve this problem later; for now we want to anticipate the following qualitative result: the final equation we will obtain will closely resemble the wave equation, and the solutions {1} will have the appearance of wave packets. So the wave that accompanies a material particle, of which we spoke in the previous section, is nothing other than the vector of probability amplitudes {2} associated with the position variable.

it: Per chiarezza ricapitoliamo i punti importanti
en: For clarity we recap the important points we have introduced:

it: Una particella materiale è caratterizzata dalla variabile posizione
en: A material particle is characterised by the position variable, that is, by a coordinate which we will briefly denote by the symbol x.

it: Lo stato di una particella materiale in un istante t
en: The state of a material particle at an instant t is represented by the distribution of probability amplitudes {0} of the variable x, which we denote by the ket symbol {1}.

it: L'evoluzione dello stato è descritta dalla seguente equazione:
en: The evolution of the state is described by the following equation:

it: L'equazione di evoluzione temporale che abbiamo scritto
en: The time-evolution equation we have written allows a finite jump between the instants t₀ and t. However, it is more convenient to consider an infinitesimal time jump t→t+dt. In this case we have the equation

it: Prima di procedere è necessario soffermarci
en: Before proceeding, we must dwell on some mathematical topics.

it: Algebra degli operatori.
en: Algebra of operators.

it: In questa scheda abbiamo introdotto vettori e matrici a dimensione infinita
en: In this card we have introduced vectors and matrices of infinite dimension. In finite-dimensional cases a vector is represented by an n-tuple of components {0}; in infinite-dimensional cases, instead, we can represent a vector by a function {1}, where the variable x takes the role of the indices. Analogously, an infinite-dimensional matrix is represented by a function of two variables {2}.

it: Il prodotto tra una matrice ed un vettore, ad esempio
en: The product of a matrix and a vector, for example, can be written by means of an integral

it: In modo analogo si possono scrivere altri tipi di prodotti
en: In an analogous way one can write other types of products between matrices or between vectors.

en: Adjoint matrix and Hermitian, anti-Hermitian and unitary matrices.

en: and suppose we have to determine the conjugate bra {0}. Consider for example the two-dimensional case

en: where the matrix {0} is obtained by conjugating and transposing the matrix A.

it: Per definizione diremo che la matrice A*t è la aggiunta
en: By definition we will say that the matrix {0} is the adjoint of the matrix A, and we will denote it with the symbol {1}

it: Ad esempio, per una matrice a dimensione due

it: In base a questa definizione possiamo scrivere che il bra coniugato
en: On the basis of this definition we can write that the conjugate bra of the ket {0} is {1}.

it: Per definizione se una matrice è uguale alla sua aggiunta
en: By definition, if a matrix is equal to its adjoint {0}, then it is said to be Hermitian; if instead the matrix is equal to its adjoint with the sign changed {1}, then it is said to be anti-Hermitian. A matrix such that {2}, where I is the identity matrix, is said to be unitary.

it: L'operazione di prendere la matrice aggiunta
en: The operation of taking the adjoint matrix, in the field of matrices, takes the role of the operation of taking the complex conjugate in the field of complex numbers. So Hermitian matrices take the role of purely real numbers, while anti-Hermitian matrices take the role of purely imaginary numbers. Unitary matrices, finally, take the role of numbers of unit modulus.

it: Le matrici hermitiane, le antihermitiane e le unitarie
en: Hermitian, anti-Hermitian and unitary matrices have very interesting properties that we will study later, and they are of considerable importance in Quantum Mechanics.

it: Rapporto tra matrici ed operatori.
en: Relation between matrices and operators.

it: Nel caso di vettori a dimensione finita sappiamo
en: In the case of finite-dimensional vectors we know, from the study of linear algebra, that any linear operator {0} can be written as a matrix product {1}, where R is a particular matrix associated with the operator in question.

it: Questa proprietà resta valida anche nel caso di vettori a dimensione infinita
en: This property remains valid also in the case of infinite-dimensional vectors, but it is much more delicate from the mathematical point of view.

it: Consideriamo ad esempio l'operatore derivata
en: Consider for example the derivative operator, which we will denote by D. The operator D transforms a vector {0} associated with the function {1} into the vector {2} associated with the function {3}

en: From now on we will speak indifferently of operators or of matrices. A derivative operator will in general be represented by the derivative operation rather than by the associated matrix; in any case it is important to know that to any linear operator there is always associated a definite matrix, whether finite- or infinite-dimensional.

it: Per determinare la matrice H sfrutteremo le informazioni
en: To determine the matrix H we will exploit the information we know from Classical Mechanics. We know that Newton's laws are very accurate over a vast range of phenomena, so we will choose the matrix H in such a way that the time-evolution law of Quantum Mechanics and Newton's law f=ma give the same results over this range of phenomena.

it: Per semplicità consideriamo un caso non molto complicato
en: For simplicity we consider a not very complicated case: we determine the Hamiltonian matrix for a charged particle in a one-dimensional space, in the presence of a time-independent electric field.

it: La diversità tra le due equazioni è dovuta al modo
en: The difference between the two equations is due to the way we represent the evolution of the system. From the classical point of view we have a material particle and must determine the equation of motion {0}. From the quantum point of view, instead, we have a system with its observable quantity x, that is, the position, and must determine the distribution of probability amplitudes {1} as time varies.

To determine this constant we must refer to an experience that lies outside the domain of Classical Mechanics, because we have already exploited all the information we could draw from that theory. Indeed, Newton's equation contains all the information of the classical theory.

it: Per determinare c useremo la relazione di De Broglie
en: To determine c we will use the De Broglie relation {0}, which we verified with the electron-diffraction experiment.

it: Consideriamo l'equazione di Schrödinger scritta per il caso
en: Consider the Schrödinger equation written for the case in which the electric potential is zero {0}

it: Questa equazione ammette soluzioni di tipo esponenziale complesso
en: This equation admits solutions of complex-exponential type

it: Che è verificata se
en: which is satisfied if {0}. So we have the solutions

it: Queste distribuzioni di ampiezze di probabilità non sono accettabili
en: These distributions of probability amplitudes are not acceptable from a physical point of view, because they give a probability of finding the particle that is constant over the whole space, from {0} to {1}

it: Tuttavia l'equazione di Schrödinger è lineare
en: However, the Schrödinger equation is linear, and so one can build other solutions by superposing the exponential ones. In this way wave packets are realised that have a limited spatial extension and are physically acceptable

it: Se ad esempio scegliamo
en: If, for example, we choose {0} we recover the exponential function

it: Questo significa che la distribuzione esponenziale rappresenta
en: This means that the exponential distribution represents a wave packet that has infinite spatial extension but a well-defined wavelength. A finite wave packet, on the other hand, cannot have such a precise wavelength because it is made of the superposition of several exponential functions, each with a different wavelength. So the exponential solution, even if it is not physically acceptable, is very convenient for representing the limiting cases of monochromatic beams, that is, with a very precise wavelength.

it: In generale sappiamo che ogni distribuzione di ampiezze di probabilità deve
en: In general we know that every distribution of probability amplitudes must be normalised, that is, divided by its own modulus. This is not possible for an exponential solution because its modulus is infinite

it: Tuttavia, pur di salvare le soluzioni esponenziali
en: However, in order to save the exponential solutions, we will use an artifice: whenever in the calculations we introduce an exponential distribution, we will write at the denominator the integral that expresses its modulus, without ever computing it, waiting for an equal integral to appear at the numerator with which it can be simplified. We will apply this artifice now, as we compute the momentum of a particle in a state associated with an exponential distribution.

it: Supponiamo di conoscere lo stato di una particella
en: Suppose we know the state of a particle and the corresponding normalised distribution of probability amplitudes {0}, and suppose we want to compute the momentum associated with this particle. In reality we have not yet defined the concept of momentum in Quantum Mechanics, but it is not hard to guess that what we want to compute is the quantity {1}, where {2} is the mean value of the random variable x. A few pages ago we wrote

it: dove X˙=i[H,X] è un operatore
en: where {0} is an operator we called the velocity operator. Let us see what form {1} takes with the H we have determined

it: Dunque possiamo scrivere la formula per
en: So we can write the formula for {0}

it: Definendo l'operatore quantità di moto
en: Defining the momentum operator {0} we have {1}.

it: Calcoliamo ora la quantità di moto per una ampiezza
en: Let us now compute the momentum for a probability amplitude with exponential distribution

it: Dove con M abbiamo indicato il modulo della distribuzione esponenziale
en: where by M we have denoted the modulus of the non-normalised exponential distribution.

it: Dunque abbiamo trovato
en: So we have found {0}.

it: Ricordando la relazione di De Broglie
en: Recalling the De Broglie relation {0}, we can conclude that it must be {1}. For convenience one defines the reduced Planck constant {2}, with which we write {3}. Substituting this value we can finally give the Schrödinger equation in its definitive form

it: Dove abbiamo usato la definizione
en: where we have used the definition {0}.

it: Nei testi di Meccanica Quantistica generalmente si trova
en: In textbooks of Quantum Mechanics one generally finds the Schrödinger equation written with the {0} on the left-hand side

it: con
en: with

it: D'ora in avanti anche noi useremo questa forma.
en: From now on we too will use this form.

it: Conclusioni.
en: Conclusions.

it: In questa scheda abbiamo trovato un'equazione che rappresenta
en: In this card we have found an equation that represents the law of evolution for a charged particle according to the formalism of Quantum Mechanics. The equation we have found does not take into account the Theory of Relativity and is, moreover, greatly simplified: it does not consider magnetic or gravitational fields and is written for a one-dimensional space. Nevertheless it is a good example that achieves the aim of this card, that is, to show the fundamental features of Quantum Mechanics applied to a system of practical interest. In the following cards we will generalise this equation and apply it to study concrete problems.
